title: Random Vectors, Random Matrices, Permuted Products, Permanents, and Diagrammatic Fun
speaker: Cris Moore

proof_stages.label: Diagrammatic tensor-contraction calculus
proof_stages.summary: The talk introduces a Feynman-inspired notation in which wires carry vector indices, vertices are tensors, internal edges are summed over, and familiar linear-algebra operations such as products and traces become diagrammatic contractions.

proof_stages.label: Random-vector moments and circuit partitions
proof_stages.summary: For a directed graph with independent random vectors at vertices, the product of edge inner products is shown, after averaging and replacing each local tensor by a sum over wirings, to be proportional to the circuit partition polynomial, with one factor of the dimension for each resulting cycle.

proof_stages.label: Algebraic estimators for the permanent
proof_stages.summary: The permanent is reviewed via transversals and matchings, the Godsil-Gutman random-sign determinant estimator is proved unbiased by cancellation of unmatched random signs, and the critical-ratio obstruction motivates complex, quaternionic, and Clifford-algebra variants.

proof_stages.label: Barvinok symmetrized determinant over matrix algebras
proof_stages.summary: To handle noncommutativity, the speaker distinguishes artificial and symmetrized determinants, chooses Gaussian d-by-d matrix algebras and a squared-trace scalarization, and states that the computable symmetrized estimators have essentially the same bad exponential critical ratio for constant d.

proof_stages.label: First-moment diagrams for the symmetrized estimator
proof_stages.summary: The expected value of the Barvinok symmetrized estimator is reduced to the permanent times a factor obtained by averaging trace covariances, replacing Gaussian matrix pairs by cup-cap tensors, counting loops in the resulting diagrams, and recognizing commutators in the symmetric group whose class functions are then analyzed by Fourier theory.

proof_stages.label: Second-moment lower bound and failure of the method
proof_stages.summary: For the critical ratio, the fourth-moment tensor becomes a sum of two combinatorial tensors, producing more complicated commutator diagrams involving products of cycles and yielding only lower bounds that show the second-moment method cannot prove concentration of the symmetrized estimator.

proof_stages.label: Permuted products in quasirandom groups
proof_stages.summary: The same cup-cap and exchange-operator calculus is applied to products of random group elements in different orders, where Schur-lemma averages over irreducible representations and loop counts give expansion and near-independence bounds governed by cycle structure.

proof_stages.label: Synthesis: averages become combinatorics
proof_stages.summary: The conclusion frames all three applications as instances where diagrammatic methods convert integrals or averages over random vectors, matrices, or group elements into combinatorial objects that can often be attacked with discrete Fourier analysis.
